A retail chain operates five stores across different cities. The table below shows the quarterly sales data (in ₹ lakhs) for each store across four quarters of a financial year:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Store} & \textbf{Q1} & \textbf{Q2} & \textbf{Q3} & \textbf{Q4} \\
\hline
Delhi & 72 & 84 & 96 & 108 \\
\hline
Mumbai & 88 & 99 & 110 & 121 \\
\hline
Bangalore & 65 & 78 & 91 & 104 \\
\hline
Hyderabad & 80 & 92 & 104 & 116 \\
\hline
Chennai & 56 & 68 & 80 & 92 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Based on the data, if the chain decides to allocate a bonus fund proportional to the annual growth rate for each store, which store would receive the \textbf{maximum bonus fund}? (Note: Growth rate is calculated as the percentage increase from Q1 to Q4)
explanation:
To solve this, we calculate the growth rate from Q1 to Q4 for each store using the formula: $$\text{Growth Rate} = \frac{Q4 - Q1}{Q1} \times 100\%$$

\textbf{Statement 1 — Delhi}: $\frac{108 - 72}{72} \times 100 = \frac{36}{72} \times 100 = 50\%$ \quad TRUE

\textbf{Statement 2 — Mumbai}: $\frac{121 - 88}{88} \times 100 = \frac{33}{88} \times 100 = 37.5\%$ \quad TRUE

\textbf{Statement 3 — Bangalore}: $\frac{104 - 65}{65} \times 100 = \frac{39}{65} \times 100 = 60\%$ \quad TRUE

\textbf{Statement 4 — Chennai}: $\frac{92 - 56}{56} \times 100 = \frac{36}{56} \times 100 \approx 64.29\%$ \quad TRUE

Comparing all growth rates in ascending order:
$$\text{Mumbai (37.5\%) < Delhi (50\%) < Bangalore (60\%) < Chennai (64.29\%)}$$

\textbf{Chennai demonstrates the highest growth rate at approximately 64.3\%}, qualifying it for the maximum bonus fund allocation. The answer is \textbf{Option D}.

\hline
Mumbai & 72 & 18 & 81 & 21 & 88 \\
\hline
Bangalore & 38 & 10 & 41 & 12 & 45 \\
\hline

Chennai & 44 & 11 & 48 & 13 & 53 \\

The store manager needs to evaluate performance. Using the given data, find the \textbf{combined efficiency ratio} (total sales per total thousand customers) for \textbf{Month 2}, considering only the stores where Month 2 footfall exceeded 15 thousand. Round your answer to one decimal place.

It reflects authentic IBPS PO multi-step application-level complexity without requiring a diagram.

text:
A multinational retail chain operates in four regions: North, South, East, and West. The table below shows the number of stores, average revenue per store (in ₹ lakhs), and employee strength per store across these regions for the financial year 2023–24.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Region} & \textbf{Number of Stores} & \textbf{Avg Revenue/Store (₹L)} & \textbf{Employees/Store} \\
\hline
North & 48 & 52 & 58 \\
\hline
South & 56 & 61 & 64 \\
\hline
East & 32 & 48 & 54 \\
\hline
West & 44 & 55 & 66 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The company plans to optimize operations by identifying regions for expansion and cost reduction. A region is considered \textbf{high-performing} if its total regional revenue exceeds ₹2800 lakhs \textbf{AND} its employee-to-revenue ratio (employees per ₹10 lakh revenue) is below 11.5. Additionally, the company will impose a 12\% efficiency tax on regions where average revenue per store is less than ₹50 lakhs.

Based on the data, \textbf{which of the following statements is correct}?

options.A: Both North and West regions qualify as high-performing; East region is subject to the efficiency tax.
options.B: Only South region qualifies as high-performing; East region is subject to the efficiency tax.
options.C: South and West regions qualify as high-performing; North region is exempt from the efficiency tax.
options.D: Only East region qualifies as high-performing; North and West regions are exempt from the efficiency tax.

metadata.subject: Preliminary Exam – Quantitative Aptitude
metadata.sub_topic: Tabular DI – Multi-Criteria Qualification with Ratio Calculations
metadata.variation_note: Corrected version with achievable thresholds. Employee-to-revenue ratio threshold (11.5) is now realistic and discriminating. Total revenue threshold (₹2800L) ensures only one region qualifies as high-performing. Numbers chosen (48, 56, 32, 44; 52, 61, 48, 55; 58, 64, 54, 66) are distinct from all previous number sets. Compound logic (AND operator) requires simultaneous verification of two independent conditions. Correct answer option (B) now matches the analytical conclusion with no internal contradiction.

 & \textbf{No.} & \textbf{Avg (L)} & \textbf{No.} & \textbf{Avg (L)} & \textbf{No.} & \textbf{Avg (L)} & \textbf{No.} & \textbf{Avg (L)} \\

 & \textbf{Prod.} & \textbf{Sales} & \textbf{Prod.} & \textbf{Sales} & \textbf{Prod.} & \textbf{Sales} \\

For company \textbf{B}, what is the ratio of the total unsold stock accumulated at the end of 2021 (i.e., unsold stock carried from 2019 + unsold from 2020 + unsold in 2021) to the total sales of company \textbf{D} across all three years?

A financial analyst reviews quarterly revenue and operational expense data for five corporate divisions. The table below shows Q3 and Q4 figures (in \textbf{₹ crore}). A division is classified as \textbf{High-Efficiency} if: (i) Revenue growth $\geq 18\%$, AND (ii) Expense-to-Revenue ratio in Q4 $\leq 0.62$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Division} & \textbf{Q3 Revenue} & \textbf{Q4 Revenue} & \textbf{Q3 Expense} & \textbf{Q4 Expense} \\
\hline
Automatic Systems & 312 & 362 & 189 & 217 \\
\hline
Biotech Solutions & 267 & 301 & 154 & 183 \\
\hline
Cybersecurity Plus & 285 & 346 & 196 & 198 \\
\hline
Digital Ventures & 223 & 256 & 138 & 169 \\
\hline
Eco Innovations & 298 & 374 & 168 & 219 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Which of the following divisions are classified as \textbf{High-Efficiency}, and what is the combined Q4 revenue of such divisions?

options.A: Cybersecurity Plus and Eco Innovations; ₹720 crore
options.B: Automatic Systems, Cybersecurity Plus, and Eco Innovations; ₹1,082 crore
options.D: Automatic Systems and Cybersecurity Plus; ₹708 crore

explanation:
\textbf{Step 1: Calculate Revenue Growth for each division}

$$\text{Revenue Growth} = \frac{Q4 \text{ Revenue} - Q3 \text{ Revenue}}{Q3 \text{ Revenue}} \times 100$$

\textbf{Automatic Systems:} $\frac{362-312}{312} \times 100 = \frac{50}{312} \times 100 = 16.03\%$ ✗ (Below 18%)

\textbf{Biotech Solutions:} $\frac{301-267}{267} \times 100 = \frac{34}{267} \times 100 = 12.73\%$ ✗ (Below 18%)

\textbf{Cybersecurity Plus:} $\frac{346-285}{285} \times 100 = \frac{61}{285} \times 100 = 21.40\%$ ✓ ($\geq 18\%$)

\textbf{Digital Ventures:} $\frac{256-223}{223} \times 100 = \frac{33}{223} \times 100 = 14.80\%$ ✗ (Below 18%)

\textbf{Eco Innovations:} $\frac{374-298}{298} \times 100 = \frac{76}{298} \times 100 = 25.50\%$ ✓ ($\geq 18\%$)

\textbf{Step 2: Calculate Q4 Expense-to-Revenue Ratio for divisions meeting criterion (i)}

\textbf{Cybersecurity Plus:} $\frac{198}{346} = 0.5723$ ✓ ($\leq 0.62$)

\textbf{Eco Innovations:} $\frac{219}{374} = 0.5856$ ✓ ($\leq 0.62$)

\textbf{Step 3: Identify High-Efficiency divisions and sum Q4 revenue}

Both \textbf{Cybersecurity Plus} and \textbf{Eco Innovations} satisfy both criteria:
- Criterion (i): Revenue growth $\geq 18\%$ — Both qualify
- Criterion (ii): Q4 Expense-to-Revenue $\leq 0.62$ — Both qualify

Combined Q4 Revenue: $346 + 374 = 720$ crore

\textbf{Why other options fail:}

\textbf{Option B — FALSE:} Automatic Systems has only $16.03\%$ growth, failing criterion (i). It cannot be High-Efficiency.

\textbf{Option C — FALSE:} Cybersecurity Plus also qualifies under both criteria. The classification cannot exclude it.

\textbf{Option D — FALSE:} Automatic Systems fails criterion (i) (16.03% $< 18\%$). Sum $362 + 346 = 708$ crore is arithmetically correct for those two, but Automatic Systems is not High-Efficiency.

text:
The bar graph below shows the number of laptops sold by five different stores (P, Q, R, S, and T) in a particular month. Study the graph carefully and answer the question.

\textbf{Store P:} 240 laptops, \textbf{Store Q:} 180 laptops, \textbf{Store R:} 300 laptops, \textbf{Store S:} 210 laptops, \textbf{Store T:} 270 laptops.

What is the \textbf{average number of laptops} sold per store across all five stores?

The bar graph below shows the number of laptops sold by five different stores (P, Q, R, S, and T) in a particular month.

\textbf{Store P:} 320 units\\
\textbf{Store Q:} 480 units\\
\textbf{Store R:} 560 units\\
\textbf{Store S:} 400 units\\
\textbf{Store T:} 640 units\\

What is the \textbf{average number of laptops} sold per store in that month?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1 — Sum of all laptops sold:}\\ $320 + 480 + 560 + 400 + 640 = 2400$ units.\\
\textbf{Step 2 — Number of stores:}\\ Total stores $= 5$.\\
\textbf{Step 3 — Calculate average:}\\ $\text{Average} = \dfrac{2400}{5} = 480$ units.\\
\textbf{Why other options are wrong:}\\ Option B ($460$) arises if a student mistakenly uses $2300 \div 5$. Option C ($500$) results from an addition error giving $2500$. Option D ($440$) comes from incorrectly summing to $2200$. The correct answer is \textbf{480}.

text:
The following bar graph shows the number of tablets sold (in units) by five stores — A, B, C, D, and E — during two months: \textbf{March} and \textbf{April}.

\textbf{What is the difference between the total tablets sold by stores A and C in March and the total tablets sold by stores B and D in April?}
explanation:
extbf{Reading values from the bar graph:}
ewline Store A (March) $= 160$ units
ewline Store C (March) $= 190$ units
ewline Store B (April) $= 145$ units
ewline Store D (April) $= 155$ units
ewline
ewline	extbf{Step 1 — Total of A and C in March:}
ewline $160 + 190 = 350$ units
ewline
ewline	extbf{Step 2 — Total of B and D in April:}
ewline $145 + 155 = 300$ units
ewline
ewline	extbf{Step 3 — Difference:}
ewline $350 - 300 = 50$ units
ewline
ewline	extbf{Why other options are incorrect:}
ewline 	extbf{Option A (30):} Results from misreading Store C as 170 instead of 190, giving $330 - 300 = 30$.
ewline 	extbf{Option B (40):} Occurs if Store A is read as 150 instead of 160, giving $150 + 190 = 340$, and $340 - 300 = 40$.
ewline 	extbf{Option D (60):} Occurs if Store B is misread as 135 instead of 145, giving $350 - 290 = 60$.

text:
The bar graph below shows the number of laptops sold (in units) by five different stores — P, Q, R, S, and T — during two months: March and April of 2024.

\textbf{March Sales (units):} P --- 230, Q --- 185, R --- 260, S --- 210, T --- 195

\textbf{April Sales (units):} P --- 275, Q --- 220, R --- 305, S --- 245, T --- 235

What is the average number of laptops sold per store in April?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1: Calculate Total April Sales across all five stores}
$$275 + 220 + 305 + 245 + 235$$
$$= 275 + 220 = 495$$
$$495 + 305 = 800$$
$$800 + 245 = 1045$$
$$1045 + 235 = 1280 \text{ units}$$

\textbf{Step 2: Calculate the Average}
$$\text{Average} = \frac{\text{Total April Sales}}{\text{Number of Stores}} = \frac{1280}{5} = 256 \text{ units}$$

\textbf{Verification of options:}

\textbf{Option A (256 units) --- TRUE:} This is the correct average as computed above.

\textbf{Option B (263 units) --- FALSE:} This results from incorrectly including March data in the April total.

\textbf{Option C (249 units) --- FALSE:} This results from omitting one store's April sales before dividing.

\textbf{Option D (271 units) --- FALSE:} This results from dividing the total by 4 instead of 5.

Hence, the correct answer is \textbf{Option A: 256 units}.

text:
A retail chain tracks monthly sales (in \textbf{Rs.} lakhs) across four product categories: \textbf{Electronics}, \textbf{Clothing}, \textbf{Home Goods}, and \textbf{Sports Equipment}. The bar graph shows sales data for six consecutive months.

\textbf{Month-wise Sales (in Rs. lakhs):}

\textbf{Electronics:} 155, 168, 182, 195, 207, 219

\textbf{Clothing:} 92, 104, 118, 131, 145, 158

\textbf{Home Goods:} 126, 138, 151, 163, 176, 188

\textbf{Sports Equipment:} 68, 79, 91, 103, 115, 127

\textbf{Question:} In which month was the combined sales of all four categories closest to Rs. 650 lakhs, and by what approximate percentage did Electronics sales exceed Sports Equipment sales in that month?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1: Calculate combined sales for each month}

Month 1: $155 + 92 + 126 + 68 = 441$ lakhs

Month 2: $168 + 104 + 138 + 79 = 489$ lakhs

Month 3: $182 + 118 + 151 + 91 = 542$ lakhs

Month 4: $195 + 131 + 163 + 103 = 592$ lakhs

Month 5: $207 + 145 + 176 + 115 = 643$ lakhs

Month 6: $219 + 158 + 188 + 127 = 692$ lakhs

\textbf{Step 2: Find the month with combined sales closest to Rs. 650 lakhs}

Month 3: $|542 - 650| = 108$

Month 4: $|592 - 650| = 58$

Month 5: $|643 - 650| = 7$ \textbf{(smallest difference)}

Month 6: $|692 - 650| = 42$

\textbf{Month 5} is closest to Rs. 650 lakhs.

\textbf{Step 3: Percentage by which Electronics exceeds Sports Equipment in Month 5}

Electronics $= 207$, Sports Equipment $= 115$

$\text{Percentage} = \dfrac{207 - 115}{115} \times 100 = \dfrac{92}{115} \times 100 \approx 80.0\%$

\textbf{Answer: Month 5; approximately 80.0\%} $\Rightarrow$ Option C

text:
A retail company tracked the sales (in lakhs Rs.) of five product categories across two quarters. Study the bar graph data below and answer the question.

\textbf{Product Sales Data (in lakhs Rs.):}

Q1 Sales: Electronics---145, Clothing---118, Home Appliances---176, Sports Gear---91, Beauty Products---127

Q2 Sales: Electronics---188, Clothing---138, Home Appliances---219, Sports Gear---115, Beauty Products---155

The company plans to allocate a marketing budget proportional to the \textbf{average growth rate} (percentage increase from Q1 to Q2) across all categories. If the total marketing budget is Rs.~85 lakhs, what is the approximate budget allocation (in lakhs Rs.) for the category with the \textbf{lowest average growth rate}?

Study the bar graph carefully and answer the question that follows.

The bar graph shows the \textbf{number of home loans} (in thousands) sanctioned by five banks — P, Q, R, S, and T — in two consecutive years, \textbf{2022} and \textbf{2023}.

\textbf{2022:} P = 72, Q = 58, R = 94, S = 63, T = 81 (all in thousands)

\textbf{2023:} P = 84, Q = 75, R = 86, S = 78, T = 69 (all in thousands)

If \textbf{12\%} of the home loans sanctioned by Bank R in 2022 and \textbf{15\%} of those sanctioned by Bank Q in 2023 were \textbf{defaulted}, and the average default amount per loan for Banks R (2022) and Q (2023) was \textbf{Rs.~8.5 lakhs} and \textbf{Rs.~6.4 lakhs} respectively, then what is the \textbf{approximate ratio} of the total default amount of Bank R in 2022 to that of Bank Q in 2023?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1 — Find number of defaulted loans:}

Bank R in 2022: $12\%$ of $94{,}000 = 0.12 \times 94{,}000 = 11{,}280$

Bank Q in 2023: $15\%$ of $75{,}000 = 0.15 \times 75{,}000 = 11{,}250$

\textbf{Step 2 — Calculate total default amount:}

Bank R (2022): $11{,}280 \times 8.5 = 95{,}880$ lakhs

Bank Q (2023): $11{,}250 \times 6.4 = 72{,}000$ lakhs

\textbf{Step 3 — Find the ratio:}

$$\frac{95{,}880}{72{,}000} = \frac{9588}{7200} = \frac{1003}{720} \approx \frac{1003}{720}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator: $\gcd$ does not simplify further neatly.

So the ratio is $\textbf{1003 : 720}$.

\textbf{Distractor analysis:}

\textbf{Option A ($133 : 96$)} — error from incorrectly taking $\frac{94 \times 8.5}{75 \times 6.4}$ without applying the percentages correctly (using same percentage).

\textbf{Option C ($1003 : 480$)} — error from using $10\%$ instead of $15\%$ for Bank Q.

\textbf{Option D ($133 : 72$)} — error from ignoring the percentage for Bank Q entirely.

The correct answer is \textbf{Option B}.

text:
Study the following bar graph carefully and answer the question below.

The bar graph shows the \textbf{number of home loans} (in hundreds) sanctioned by five branches — P, Q, R, S, and T — of a bank during \textbf{2021} and \textbf{2022}.

\textbf{2021:} P = 34, Q = 46, R = 52, S = 28, T = 41

\textbf{2022:} P = 43, Q = 38, R = 61, S = 47, T = 36

(All figures are in hundreds.)

\textbf{Question:} The total number of home loans sanctioned by branches P and S together in 2022 is what percentage more or less than the total number of home loans sanctioned by branches Q and T together in 2021? (Round to one decimal place.)
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1:} Find total home loans for branches P and S together in \textbf{2022}.
$$P_{2022} + S_{2022} = 43 + 47 = 90 \text{ (in hundreds)}$$

\textbf{Step 2:} Find total home loans for branches Q and T together in \textbf{2021}.
$$Q_{2021} + T_{2021} = 46 + 41 = 87 \text{ (in hundreds)}$$

\textbf{Step 3:} Since $90 > 87$, the value in 2022 is \textbf{more}. Calculate the percentage difference:
$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{90 - 87}{87} \times 100 = \frac{3}{87} \times 100 = \frac{300}{87} \approx 3.448\%$$

Rounded to one decimal place: $\approx 3.4\%$ \textbf{more}.

\textbf{Common errors:}
- Choosing option B: Correct magnitude but wrong direction (less instead of more).
- Choosing option C ($4.6\%$ more): Dividing by the wrong base, i.e., using $\frac{3}{65} \times 100$ or a miscalculation such as $\frac{4}{87} \times 100$.
- Choosing option D ($4.6\%$ less): Both wrong direction and wrong base.

The correct answer is \textbf{(A) $3.4\%$ more}.

text:
Study the following bar graph carefully and answer the question below.

The bar graph shows the \textbf{number of loan applications received} and the \textbf{number of loan applications sanctioned} by a bank across \textbf{five zones} (P, Q, R, S, T) during the financial year 2023--24.

\textbf{Zone P:} Received $= 750$, Sanctioned $= 510$

\textbf{Zone Q:} Received $= 640$, Sanctioned $= 448$

\textbf{Zone R:} Received $= 880$, Sanctioned $= 572$

\textbf{Zone S:} Received $= 560$, Sanctioned $= 392$

\textbf{Zone T:} Received $= 720$, Sanctioned $= 504$

The bank decides to \textbf{upgrade} any zone where the \textbf{rejection rate} (i.e., percentage of applications \textit{not} sanctioned out of total received) is \textbf{strictly greater than} the \textbf{average rejection rate across all five zones}. For each upgraded zone, the bank allocates an additional budget of Rs.$\,4.5$ lakh per rejected application in that zone. What is the \textbf{total additional budget} (in Rs. lakh) allocated to all upgraded zones combined?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1: Find rejected applications per zone.}

\begin{itemize}
\item Zone P: $750 - 510 = 240$
\item Zone Q: $640 - 448 = 192$
\item Zone R: $880 - 572 = 308$
\item Zone S: $560 - 392 = 168$
\item Zone T: $720 - 504 = 216$
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2: Find rejection rate per zone.}

\begin{itemize}
\item Zone P: $\frac{240}{750} \times 100 = 32\%$
\item Zone Q: $\frac{192}{640} \times 100 = 30\%$
\item Zone R: $\frac{308}{880} \times 100 = 35\%$
\item Zone S: $\frac{168}{560} \times 100 = 30\%$
\item Zone T: $\frac{216}{720} \times 100 = 30\%$
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 3: Average rejection rate (weighted).}

Total received $= 750 + 640 + 880 + 560 + 720 = 3550$

Total rejected $= 240 + 192 + 308 + 168 + 216 = 1124$

Average rejection rate $= \frac{1124}{3550} \times 100 \approx 31.66\%$

\textbf{Step 4: Identify upgraded zones} (rejection rate $> 31.66\%$).

\textbf{Zone P} ($32\%$) and \textbf{Zone R} ($35\%$) are strictly above the average.

\textbf{Step 5: Compute additional budget.}

Rejected in Zone P $= 240$, Rejected in Zone R $= 308$.

Total rejected in upgraded zones $= 240 + 308 = 548$.

Total additional budget $= 548 \times 4.5 = 2{,}466$ lakh.

\textbf{Option A ($3{,}510$):} Error from including all zones with $\geq 30\%$ rejection.
\textbf{Option C ($4{,}032$):} Error from miscalculating rejection counts.
\textbf{Option D ($1{,}386$):} Error from including only Zone R.

The correct answer is \textbf{B: Rs. $2{,}466$ lakh}.

text:
A retail chain analyzed sales performance across five product categories over two quarters. The bar graph below shows the quarterly sales (in Rs. lakhs) for each category.

\begin{enumerate}
\item Category A: Q1 = 156, Q2 = 198
\item Category B: Q1 = 174, Q2 = 216
\item Category C: Q1 = 132, Q2 = 159
\item Category D: Q1 = 189, Q2 = 234
\item Category E: Q1 = 147, Q2 = 171
\end{enumerate}

The management has set a target: any category with a Q1-to-Q2 growth rate \textbf{exceeding 22\%} receives a performance bonus. The bonus pool is limited to Rs.~45 lakhs total. If bonuses are distributed \textbf{proportionally to excess growth} (i.e., growth above 22\%), what is the bonus amount allocated to the category with the \textbf{second-highest excess growth rate}?

explanation:
We need to find the total laptops sold in \textbf{February} and \textbf{April} together.

\textbf{Step 1:} Laptops sold in February $= 310$

\textbf{Step 2:} Laptops sold in April $= 390$

\textbf{Step 3:} Total $= 310 + 390 = \textbf{700}$

\textbf{Common error traps:}
\begin{itemize}
\item Option A ($680$): Could result from misreading April as $370$ instead of $390$.
\item Option C ($665$): Could result from picking March ($275$) instead of April ($390$).
\item Option D ($720$): Could result from misreading February as $330$.
\end{itemize}

The correct answer is \textbf{(B) $700$}.

text:
The line graph below shows the number of laptops sold (in units) by a store over six months (January to June). Study the graph carefully and answer the question.

\textbf{What is the total number of laptops sold in the months of February, April, and June combined?}

The line graph below shows the \textbf{monthly sales revenue (in lakhs)} of a company from January to June 2024. Study the graph carefully and answer the question.

\textbf{Monthly Sales Revenue (in lakhs):} Jan $= 40$, Feb $= 50$, Mar $= 45$, Apr $= 65$, May $= 55$, Jun $= 70$

By what percentage did the sales revenue \textbf{increase} from \textbf{February to April}?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1 — Identify the relevant values:}\\ Revenue in February $= 50$ lakhs\\ Revenue in April $= 65$ lakhs\\
\textbf{Step 2 — Calculate percentage increase:}\\ $\text{Percentage Increase} = \dfrac{\text{April} - \text{February}}{\text{February}} \times 100$\\
$= \dfrac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \dfrac{15}{50} \times 100 = 30\%$\\
\textbf{Why other options are wrong:}\\ \textbf{Option A ($20\%$):} A common error — computing $\frac{65-50}{65} \times 100 \approx 23\%$, close to $20\%$.\\ \textbf{Option B ($25\%$):} Incorrect base or rounding error.\\ \textbf{Option D ($35\%$):} Overestimate of the difference.\\
\textbf{Correct Answer: C} — The sales revenue increased by $\mathbf{30\%}$ from February to April.

\textbf{Given Information:}\\
Revenue in April $= 48$ lakhs\\
Revenue in May $= 36$ lakhs

\textbf{Formula for Percentage Decrease:}
$$\text{Percentage Decrease} = \frac{\text{Old Value} - \text{New Value}}{\text{Old Value}} \times 100$$

\textbf{Calculation:}
$$= \frac{48 - 36}{48} \times 100 = \frac{12}{48} \times 100 = \frac{1}{4} \times 100 = 25\%$$

\textbf{Why other options are wrong:}\\
\textbf{Option B ($20\%$):} $48 \times 0.20 = 9.6$, so new value $= 38.4 \neq 36$ \texttimes\\
\textbf{Option C ($33.3\%$):} This would be percentage decrease if old value were 54, not 48 \texttimes\\
\textbf{Option D ($16.7\%$):} $48 \times \frac{1}{6} = 8$, so new value $= 40 \neq 36$ \texttimes\\
\textbf{Option A ($25\%$):} $\frac{12}{48} \times 100 = 25\%$ \checkmark

\textbf{Answer: A $-$ 25\%}

text:
The line graph below shows the \textbf{monthly revenue (in \rupee~lakhs)} earned by two online platforms --- \textbf{Platform A} and \textbf{Platform B} --- over five months from \textbf{January to May 2024}.

\textbf{Platform A:} Jan = 80, Feb = 95, Mar = 110, Apr = 100, May = 125

\textbf{Platform B:} Jan = 60, Feb = 80, Mar = 90, Apr = 120, May = 105

What is the ratio of the \textbf{average revenue of Platform A} over the five months to the \textbf{percentage increase in Platform B's revenue} from January to May?
explanation:
\textbf{Step 1 --- Average Revenue of Platform A:}

Total revenue of Platform A $= 80 + 95 + 110 + 100 + 125 = 510$ lakhs.

Average $= \dfrac{510}{5} = 102$ lakhs.

\textbf{Step 2 --- Percentage Increase in Platform B from January to May:}

January revenue $= 60$ lakhs, May revenue $= 105$ lakhs.

Increase $= 105 - 60 = 45$ lakhs.

Percentage increase $= \dfrac{45}{60} \times 100 = 75\%$.

\textbf{Step 3 --- Required Ratio:}

Ratio $= \dfrac{\text{Average of A}}{\text{\% increase of B}} = \dfrac{102}{75}$.

\textbf{Common Errors:}

\textbf{Option B} ($82/75$) arises if students compute the average of Platform B instead of A.

\textbf{Option C} ($102/7.5$) arises if students divide the percentage by 10 accidentally.

\textbf{Option D} ($82/7.5$) combines both errors above.

Hence, the correct answer is $\dfrac{102}{75}$.

text:
Study the following line graph carefully and answer the question below.

The line graph shows the \textbf{number of home loan applications received} (in hundreds) by two branches — \textbf{Branch P} and \textbf{Branch Q} — of a bank during six consecutive months from \textbf{January to June 2019}.

\textbf{Branch P:} Jan $= 18$, Feb $= 26$, Mar $= 33$, Apr $= 21$, May $= 39$, Jun $= 29$

\textbf{Branch Q:} Jan $= 24$, Feb $= 15$, Mar $= 30$, Apr $= 42$, May $= 27$, Jun $= 36$

\textbf{Question:} If \textbf{Branch P} approved $60\%$ of its applications in March and May combined, and \textbf{Branch Q} approved $75\%$ of its applications in April and June combined, then the total number of applications \textbf{NOT approved} by Branch P (in March and May combined) is what percentage of the total number of applications \textbf{NOT approved} by Branch Q (in April and June combined)? (Round to the nearest integer.)
explanation:
All values are in hundreds, but since we need a percentage (ratio), the unit cancels out.

\textbf{Step 1 — Branch P (March + May):}
Applications in March $= 33$ (hundreds), May $= 39$ (hundreds).
Combined $= 33 + 39 = 72$ (hundreds).
Approved $= 60\%$ of $72 = 43.2$.
\textbf{Not approved by P} $= 72 - 43.2 = 28.8$ (hundreds).

\textbf{Step 2 — Branch Q (April + June):}
Applications in April $= 42$ (hundreds), June $= 36$ (hundreds).
Combined $= 42 + 36 = 78$ (hundreds).
Approved $= 75\%$ of $78 = 58.5$.
\textbf{Not approved by Q} $= 78 - 58.5 = 19.5$ (hundreds).

\textbf{Step 3 — Required percentage:}
$$\frac{28.8}{19.5} \times 100$$

Simplify: $\frac{28.8}{19.5} = \frac{288}{195} = \frac{96}{65} \approx 1.4769$

$$\approx 147.69\% \approx \textbf{148\%}$$

\textbf{Common errors:}
- Using approved figures instead of not-approved → leads to different answers like $145\%$ or $156\%$.
- Forgetting to subtract from total → gives a wrong ratio.

The correct answer is \textbf{(B) $148\%$}.

text:
The line graph below shows the \textbf{monthly sales (in thousands of units)} of two products — \textbf{Product P} and \textbf{Product Q} — across six months (January to June) for a company.

\textbf{Product P:} Jan = 60, Feb = 75, Mar = 90, Apr = 80, May = 70, Jun = 85

\textbf{Product Q:} Jan = 50, Feb = 55, Mar = 65, Apr = 95, May = 85, Jun = 60

The selling price of Product P is \textbf{\rupee 120 per unit} and of Product Q is \textbf{\rupee 150 per unit}. The cost price of Product P is \textbf{\rupee 95 per unit} and of Product Q is \textbf{\rupee 120 per unit}.

What is the \textbf{total profit earned} (in \rupee thousands) from \textbf{both products combined} during the months in which \textbf{Product Q's sales strictly exceeded Product P's sales}?
